setup.context: Teaching a dog to do tricks

root.narration: You are programming a robot dog to do a trick show! The robot dog needs to spin in a circle 3 times. You could write 'spin' three separate times, OR you could use a shortcut. Which set of instructions is the BEST way to make the dog spin 3 times?

choices.text: Repeat 3 times: spin in a circle.
Spin. Spin. Spin. (Write it out three times.)
Repeat forever: spin in a circle.

outcome.headline: Loop Master! Short and Smart!
outcome.explanation: You used a LOOP! A loop says 'do this thing' and 'how many times to do it.' Instead of writing the same instruction over and over, you just say 'repeat 3 times.' This is like telling someone 'clap your hands 5 times' instead of saying 'clap, clap, clap, clap, clap.'
outcome.whatToRemember: A loop repeats an action a certain number of times. It saves you from writing the same thing over and over!

keyTerms.definition: An instruction that tells the computer to repeat something a certain number of times.

outcome.headline: It Works! But There is a Better Way!
outcome.explanation: Writing 'spin' three times does work -- the dog spins 3 times! But what if you wanted the dog to spin 100 times? You would have to write 'spin' 100 times! A loop lets you just say 'repeat 100 times: spin.' Much easier!
outcome.whatToRemember: When you need to do the same thing many times, use a loop instead of writing it out over and over.

outcome.headline: Dizzy Dog! It Will Not Stop!
outcome.explanation: You told the dog to spin FOREVER! It will never stop spinning! That is called an infinite loop -- it goes on and on with no end. You always need to tell a loop WHEN to stop. The dog is so dizzy now!
outcome.whatToRemember: Always give your loop a number so it knows when to stop. 'Repeat forever' means it will NEVER end!
